schools.summary: 'The third day' is counted from Achish's dismissal, not the departure from Ziklag; the Amalekites raided the unguarded Negeb in revenge for David's own incursions.
Matthew Henry: the disaster is providence — Saul's spared Amalek recoils, David is corrected, yet God restrains the raiders from bloodshed.
Gill reckons the eighty-eight-mile march home and dates the third day from the parting with Achish.
Wesley glosses 'the south' as the southern part of Judah and the adjacent country.

debates.question: From what point is 'the third day' reckoned?

sides.position: from David's dismissal by Achish in Philistia, not his leaving Ziklag

schools.summary: The women and children were spared not from pity but as marketable captives, to be sold as slaves in Egypt, with whom Amalek traded.
Gill: only women and children were there to seize, the men being gone with David, so the town lay defenceless.
Henry: God inclined the raiders to spare rather than kill — a hidden restraint bending Amalek to correct, not destroy, David's house.
A terrible homecoming: charred, silent ruins, with no survivor left to tell the tale — the story must await the abandoned Egyptian slave.
Gill: they came to the place where Ziklag had stood, now lying in ruins.
Henry: the going out may be cheerful and the coming in doleful; we cannot foresee what tidings meet us at home.
The verse is the climax of the long Hebrew sentence; Clarke, from the Vulgate, marks that they wept till their very tears failed.
Wesley: it is no disparagement to the bravest spirits to lament the calamities of friends and relations.
Henry: the naming of David's two wives shows this loss went nearer his heart than any other.

themes: before the parting of the ways

schools.summary: Gill: the capture of David's two wives is noted as one special cause of his particular distress.
Henry: the naming of Ahinoam and Abigail shows this loss went nearer David's heart than all else.
Keil and Delitzsch set the private loss just before the public mutiny, stacking David's grief on the camp's danger.
Ellicott traces the mutiny to the men's degradation under Achish and David's failure to guard Ziklag; David's trust contrasts with Saul on Gilboa.
The men blamed David as author of their miseries; he encouraged himself in the God who was his by covenant, promise, and fatherly love.
Gill: David is pressed at once by the loss of his wives and by the murmuring of his men.
Henry: while the men gave liberty to their passions, David set his graces to work — at his wits' end but not his faith's end.
Ellicott laments David's long neglect of the Urim through his wanderings, yet defends the reality of the oracle against modern explaining-away.
Henry: the trouble corrects David's earlier neglect to enquire of God; afflictions remind us of neglected duties and quicken us to seek the Lord.
Wesley: 'bring the ephod' = put it on and enquire by God's ordinance; David feels his former error and now his duty meets with success.
Gill: Abiathar, who fled from Nob with the ephod, is now high priest; the disaster stirs David to the diligence he had lacked.
Wesley: unlike the slower answer at Keilah, God replies at once because the case required haste — he suits himself to the degree of our need.
Henry: God answers above what David asked, adding unbidden the promise that he should recover all.
Gill: the inquiry was made through Abiathar, who bore David's questions to the LORD.

themes: God suits the answer to the need

schools.summary: Clarke and Ellicott map the brook Besor and explain the two hundred's collapse from a hurried, scantily-provisioned forced march; the mention is proleptic.
Gill: the six hundred set out encouraged by the oracle of the LORD.
Henry: David went himself in person, taking with him all the force he had.
Gill: pursuing with four hundred an enemy who could lose four hundred on camels and remain a host was a real trial of David's faith.
Wesley: four hundred was a small number for such an attempt, but David was strong in faith, giving God the glory.
Henry: to press on confiding in divine power is believing against hope in hope; David's sparing of the weak prefigures the Son of David's tenderness.
Ellicott: the Egyptian was a slave of the nomad Amalekites, cruelly abandoned without food or water though they had camels to carry him.
Henry: God uses a cast-off slave to confound the wise; the master's cruelty recoils, and David's mercy to a dying nobody opens the rescue.
Gill: they found him half-starved and near death, lying in the field.
The revived slave reports the raid on the Crethites, Judah, and Caleb; the three-day start made the Egyptian's guidance the difference between hopeless and possible.
Gill: the figs were dried and pressed into cakes, the concentrated food of travellers.
Wesley and Clarke: providence made the guide an Egyptian (sparable) not an Amalekite (under the ban), and turned the master's cruelty into his own ruin.
Gill: David's questions open the interrogation on which the whole pursuit depends.
The Cherethites are southern Philistines, perhaps of Cretan origin (cf. Caphtor), later David's body-guard under Benaiah; the raid swept the Negeb and burned Ziklag in revenge.
Gill: the Cherethites are the Philistines; 'Caleb' explains the district of the south belonging to Caleb's posterity.
Wesley: the Cherethites are the Philistines, and 'the south of Caleb' is added to specify that part of southern Judah.
The oath is 'by Elohim,' the God the Egyptian knew; his precise knowledge marks him a man of ability, perhaps a captured trader who knew the raiders' plan.
Henry: the poor Egyptian's high regard for the obligation of an oath, sworn by the one supreme God, is a quiet rebuke to nominal faith.
Wesley: his master had told him the intended route; Clarke: the versions supply that David swore, as the text implies.
Gill: David asks the man to direct him to where the raiders were.
Ellicott: a vivid picture of barbarian license; the veterans, remembering the desolate homes, fell with fury; Keil notes the day-long slaughter.
Henry: sinners are nearest ruin when they cry peace and safety; sensuality is the soft road to the congregation of the dead.
Wesley: secure near their own borders, they saw no danger yet were nearest destruction; Clarke cautions against over-vindicating David.
The 'twilight' is read as morning (Josephus) or evening (Targum, Keil, reading nesheph as dusk); the day-long slaughter shows how vast the host was, leaving only four hundred on camels.
Gill surveys the readings of 'twilight' and notes that as many escaped on camels as David had men, proving the disproportion of the forces.

debates.question: Does 'from the twilight' mean the morning or the evening dusk?

sides.position: the morning: David let them sleep off the feast and struck at dawn
the evening: he fell on them that night and slew till the next evening

schools.summary: Gill: David recovered all — the wives, children, and goods — save only the provisions the raiders had eaten.
Keil and Delitzsch: two wives, all the children great and small, and the booty recovered, so that nothing was missing.
Henry: God is better than our fears — Amalek's greed preserved all for the right owners; David, like Abraham, took the prey from the mighty, a type of Christ leading captivity captive.
Gill: not one captive was missing, small or great, for the raiders had carried them off alive and now all were brought back.
Keil and Delitzsch: the heaped negatives before 'David recovered all' match, fact for fact, the oracle's promise of recovering all.
The obscure verse means the Amalekite flocks were driven ahead of the recovered cattle; David chose them as his share to give as presents to Judah's friends.
Wesley: the men who had spoken of stoning David now magnify him and celebrate his praise upon this success.
Henry: those who lately would stone him now cry him up because they gained by him — the world's sentiments are governed by interest.
Keil and Delitzsch: returning to the two hundred at the Besor, David is met by them and heartily greets them.
Henry: David is just and kind to those who abode by the stuff, saluting the faint, asking them of peace, and bidding them be of good cheer.

themes: rejoicing in a work one could not share

schools.summary: Ellicott: the compiler preserved this act of greed because David's ruling became an authoritative, long-lived declaration, like the law of Moses; the four hundred grudged the spoil.
Henry: even a praying leader has men of Belial among his followers; their claim was both covetous and barbarous, giving the mouths without the meat.
Gill: they were ill-natured and covetous; Clarke: 'men of Belial' names the sour, rugged, and profane.
David grounds his ruling in the LORD's gift; Ellicott notes Ewald's ejaculatory-oath reading and the Roman parallel of Scipio dividing spoil after New Carthage.
Wesley: David overrules with sweetness, calling them brethren; what God freely imparts we must not injuriously withhold from our brethren.
Gill and Clarke: David deals gently yet keeps his authority, attributing the victory wholly to God against a far greater host.

themes: the victory is the LORD's gift

schools.summary: Wesley: the equal division is prudent and just — the guard shared the hazard, formed a reserve, and kept the station the general assigned.
Keil and Delitzsch read the idiom to mean fighter and baggage-guard shall share together, against the greed of the men of Belial.
Gill: no wise or just man would side with excluding the brethren from the spoil.
Ellicott turns the ordinance into a parable of grace — Milton's 'they also serve,' Moses praying on the hill, the Simeons and Annas who fight with prayers and tears and share the reward.
Gill notes Jarchi's tradition tracing the custom to Abraham's days, though Josephus makes David its author.
Clarke: the rule is just and has older precedent (Numbers 31; Joshua 22); 'unto this day' shows the book was composed long after.

themes: they also serve who only stand and wait

schools.summary: The spoil must have been enormous; the gifts went to the elders who sheltered David in exile and, Keil notes, disposed them to receive him as king after Saul's death.
Wesley: David gave partly in gratitude for former favours, partly in policy to engage the elders' affections.
Gill: David seems to have meant to make Ziklag his residence again; Clarke: gratitude required these presents to his old protectors.

themes: gifts to the elders of Judah
paving the road to the throne

schools.summary: South Ramoth (Ramah of the South, Shimei's likely home) and Jattir (Attir in the Judean hills) are fixed in the far south; the list maps David's places of exile.
Gill takes 'Bethel' for Kirjath-jearim, the house of God where the ark stood, not the northern Bethel.

debates.question: Which town is the 'Bethel' that received David's gift?

sides.position: Kirjath-jearim, called Bethel because the ark was there
Bethul/Bethuel in the tribe of Simeon (LXX 'Baithsour')
a Beth-el in the tribe of Ephraim

schools.summary: Ellicott fixes Aroer south of Hebron and Eshtemoa at Semua; via Bishop Hervey he shows David's later officers (Zabdi the Shiphmite, Ezri the Ramathite) came from these friend-towns of his youth.
Gill places Aroer in Judah, not the trans-Jordan city where David never went.

sides.position: an Aroer in Judah, south of Hebron
Aroer beyond Jordan on the Arnon

themes: friends of his youth made officers

schools.summary: Rachal is unknown (LXX offers five other names); the Jerahmeelites and Kenites are southern border peoples David had moved among, now untraceable.
Gill: Rachal is a city of Judah named nowhere else; Clarke floats Calmet's guess that it is Hachilah, a place of David's hiding.
Hormah is the old Zephath (modern Zep-ata), Chor-ashan probably Ashan of Judah later ceded to Simeon, and Athach is unknown, perhaps a misspelling of Ether.
Clarke recalls Hormah as the city of Arad devoted to destruction under the ban, whence its name.

themes: the name 'destruction' beside a gift of grace

schools.summary: Hebron is the ancient 'friend of God' city over the Cave of Machpelah; the list widens to all David's haunts and shows the spoil's vastness, just before his anointing there.
Gill: Hebron, a city of refuge in the Judean hills, is where David was afterward anointed king first over Judah and then over Israel.
Clarke: Hebron was strongly attached to David, where he reigned over seven years; the catalogue proves both his gratitude and the enormous spoil, and dates these events before Endor and Gilboa.

themes: from the ashes to the throne
